title: Consumption Commitments and Risk Preferences
abstract: Many households devote a large fraction of their budgets to “consumption commitments”—goods that involve transaction costs and are infrequently adjusted. This paper characterizes risk preferences in an expected utility model with commitments. We show that commitments affect risk preferences in two ways: (1) they amplify risk aversion with respect to moderate-stake shocks, and (2) they create a motive to take large-payoff gambles. The model thus helps resolve two basic puzzles in expected utility theory: the discrepancy between moderate-stake and large-stake risk aversion and lottery playing by insurance buyers. We discuss applications of the model such as the optimal design of social insurance and tax policies, added worker effects in labor supply, and portfolio choice. Using event studies of unemployment shocks, we document evidence consistent with the consumption adjustment patterns implied by the model.
summary_en: Households commit to big, slow-to-adjust expenses — mortgage payments, car loans, long-term rental leases. This paper shows that such commitments reshape how risk-averse people are in a surprising way: they make households very averse to moderate losses (because you can't easily cut back), but willing to take large-payoff gambles (because a big win lets you escape the commitment). This unifies two puzzles that have plagued expected-utility theory: why people insure small risks too heavily and buy lottery tickets at the same time. The authors document the mechanism empirically using unemployment shocks and consumption responses.
data_used: US Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) household panel data 1968–2003 combined with event-study analysis of unemployment shocks and subsequent consumption-category adjustments (housing, cars, food); ~8,000 households.
policy_relevance: Reframes the economic case for social insurance: households with consumption commitments benefit disproportionately from unemployment insurance and short-term income support, because a short shock can force costly lumpy adjustments (foreclosure, mortgage default). For the Hungarian Ministry of Finance / Ministry of Labour, supports programmes that smooth income over short unemployment spells rather than push households into permanent dissaving. Setting is US; the framework applies wherever household commitments are significant.
